Indian Central Bank Holds Rates as Mideast War Keeps Outlook in Flux -- Update
data.description:
By Fabiana Negrin Ochoa and Kimberley Kao


India's central bank kept its policy rate steady in a widely expected decision as policymakers around the world gauge how to react to the rapidly evolving war in the Middle East.

Right before the Reserve Bank of India stepped up to the plate to announce its decision on Wednesday, it seemed that the situation had taken a tentative turn for the better. President Trump and Iran announced a temporary cease-fire, spurring a relief rally across Asian markets. But analysts warn that a huge degree of uncertainty remains, and the truce is fragile.

Against that backdrop, the RBI voted unanimously to hold the policy repo rate at 5.25%, pausing for a second time after delivering a rate cut in December. The RBI also voted to keep its monetary policy stance neutral.

RBI Gov. Sanjay Malhotra said the monetary board had judged it prudent to wait and see how growth and inflation develop, warning that the war is likely to impair economic activity.

All economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal had expected Wednesday's decision, as a period of low inflation gives India some room to absorb an energy driven-price shock.

The wait-and-see approach taken by the RBI is one that has been favored by many of its peers in the region too. Earlier in the day, New Zealand's central bank also stood pat on rates, a decision it said balances the potential benefits of responding to inflation risk pre-emptively against the cost of unnecessarily stifling economic recovery.

Central banks in Indonesia, Taiwan and the Philippines have also held steady in their first decisions since the war broke out, even as inflationary risks had spurred some expectations of a hawkish shift.

In its statement, the RBI struck a cautious tone on inflation, saying that core measures remain muted but that risks stemming from the conflict render the outlook uncertain.

Analysts' takeaway from the meeting is that an extended rate pause seems likely, for now.

India's central bank is unlikely to pull the trigger on rate hikes unless consumer inflation durably surpasses 6% and inflation expectations become untethered, said Garima Kapoor at Elara Capital.

"Ultimately, the policy outlook will depend on how the crisis evolves," said Capital Economics' Gareth Leather.

If the cease-fire holds and energy prices stabilize, the RBI is likely to see any impact on the economy as temporary. But in a more adverse scenario where inflation rises well above target and the rupee weakens further, policymakers would be more inclined to tighten policy despite the potential hit to growth, Leather said.

Even if a resolution is struck and the conflict ends, unwinding the economic damage won't happen overnight, analysts say. The surge in energy prices, spillover into other commodities and the hit to consumer and business confidence will take time to normalize.

Noting the various ways through which war-driven supply-chain disruptions and higher input costs could ripple out across India's economy, the RBI said it would stay alert to how those impacts start to manifest.

Global economic conditions and sentiment have soured, and that has dimmed the growth-inflation outlook, the RBI said.

"We shall remain vigilant of the evolving situation," Malhotra said, warning that the initial supply shock could turn into a demand shock if the restoration of supply chains is delayed.


Write to Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com and Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-08-26 0326ET

General Motors recalls over 270,000 cars in US over rearview camera issue
data.description:
April 8 (Reuters) - General Motors is recalling 271,770 cars in the U.S. over an issue with the rearview camera, the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) said on Wednesday. 

Here are some details: 

* Recall includes Chevrolet Malibu sedans, NHTSA said. * The rearview camera screen may display a distorted orblank image, reducing the driver's view behind the vehicle,according to the NHTSA.  * The agency said that dealers will inspect and replace therearview camera.

(Reporting by Chandni Shah in Bengaluru; Editing by Rashmi Aich)

data.title: RBI FX curbs on positions of banks, NDFs will not be permanent, governor says
data.description:
MUMBAI, April 8 (Reuters) - The central bank's recent curbs on position limits of lenders and barring them from offering non-deliverable forwards, aimed at reining in rupee volatility, will not remain in place indefinitely, Governor Sanjay Malhotra said on Wednesday.

In a post-monetary policy briefing, Malhotra said the central bank had observed heightened volatility in the foreign exchange market in recent weeks, with banks building positions, including arbitrage trades between the onshore deliverable and offshore NDF markets.

In normal conditions, these linkages play an important role in efficient price discovery, which is why the RBI has been trying to broaden access to the NDF market, he said.

However, when volatility becomes excessive and positions build to levels that may not aid price discovery, authorities take such measures, he said.

He added that the measures were a reaction to specific market movements and did not signal any structural change, adding that the central bank remains committed to broadening and deepening these markets and to the internationalisation of the rupee.

"So obviously these measures are not going to remain in place forever," he said.

(Reporting by Nimesh Vora; Editing by Sonia Cheema)

data.title: Spanish PM Sanchez to visit China April 11-15, Chinese foreign ministry says
data.description:
BEIJING, April 8 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez will visit China from April 11 to April 15, the Chinese foreign ministry said on Wednesday, in what will be his fourth trip to the world's second-largest economy in as many years.

(Reporting by Ryan Woo and Ethan Wang; Editing by Himani Sarkar)

German Factory Orders Returned to Growth Ahead of Iran War -- 2nd Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl


German manufacturing orders snapped back to mild growth in February, ahead of the energy-price shock of the war in Iran.

Factory orders climbed 0.9% on month, recovering a little from the 11.1% slump in the first month of the year, Germany's statistics agency Destatis said Wednesday. January's decline had been driven by a fall in large-scale orders--those valued at more than 50 million euros, or $58 million--which continued to be weak in February. A consensus of economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected 1.0% overall growth in orders.

The decline in January came after orders surged toward the end of 2025, with on-month growth peaking at 6.4% in December, the fastest rate since 2024. Orders were almost 10% higher in the final quarter of last year than in the third, as the economy grew in 2025 for the first time since 2022. That came as a fiscal stimulus of more than $1 trillion pledged last year by Germany's government for defense and infrastructure investments started to seep through into industrial orders.

The car industry was the main driver of increased orders in February, alongside textiles and metals. Transport equipment, including aircraft, ships and trains, offset some of that growth.

Germany's large industrial base had traditionally been the driver of the country's economic growth. However, the sector has been mired in a recession since the late 2010s, as the country's exports faltered. Manufacturing was hit by the challenges of increased competition from China, a difficult transition to electric vehicles in its car industry, and soaring energy prices after Russia's full-scale Ukraine invasion.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the war in Iran has raised oil and gas prices again. The world's largest chemicals producer by revenue, Ludwigshafen-based BASF, as well as German firms Lanxess and Wacker Chemie, have said they are raising prices in response to higher raw-material costs.

Rising inflation more generally could prompt the European Central Bank to hike borrowing costs, adding further pressure on customers' willingness to place orders. Inflation in Germany jumped to 2.8% in March, from 2.0% in February, according to EU-harmonized data.

The bank might have to raise its key rate multiple times this year if the energy shock proves long-lasting, Belgium's central bank said this week.

"The way I feel comfortable putting it is, if this is not done by June, I think we're going to have to hike, but I don't want to exclude a hike in April," Pierre Wunsch told The Wall Street Journal's What's News podcast.

Investors anticipate nearly three ECB rate rises this year, though they pared expectations of a quarter-point hike as soon as its next meeting on April 30 after news of a two-week ceasefire on Monday.

The data suggests there is little hope for the time being that industry will significantly ramp up production in the short term and thereby boost the growth of the German economy, Commerzbank senior economist Ralph Solveen said in a note.

"This is all the more true given that the war in Iran has caused additional uncertainty, which is likely to slow investment - at least temporarily - especially since energy prices are expected to remain higher than before the war began," he said.


Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-08-26 0314ET

Danone, Eramet, Engie, LDC, Rémy Cointreau... stocks to watch today in Paris
data.description:
Alstom
In a brief press release, Alstom announced that it has been awarded a new signaling contract within the Europe region for approximately 295 million euros. The specialist in smart and sustainable mobility will book this order in the fourth quarter of its 2025/2026 fiscal year.

Danone
Danone is "discreetly studying with the American investment bank Centerview Partners" a takeover of the Reckitt subsidiary, according to La Lettre. The British firm had put 17 billion dollars on the table in 2017 to acquire Mead Johnson. However, nearly 10 years later, the diversification project has failed to meet its objectives. Write-downs, disposals, and lawsuits in the United States have marked recent years, creating an interesting opportunity for Danone. It should be noted, however, that this rumor resurfaces periodically.

DMS
The French medtech Diagnostic Medical Systems announced it has obtained European MDR 5 certification (Medical Device Regulation - EU Regulation 2017/745) for its Stratos and Stratos DR bone densitometry systems. For DMS Group, this validation not only ensures the continued marketing of its systems in Europe but also attests to the quality of its "Made in France" production.

emeis
emeis' 2025 revenue rose by 6.1% on a like-for-like basis to nearly 5,895 million euros. The momentum is mainly driven by nursing homes (nearly 2/3 of the group's activity), where revenue grew by 8.1% organically, fueled by a significant recovery in the average occupancy rate (up 2 percentage points over 12 months). The occupancy rate rose by an average of 1.8 points over one year to 87.6%: a favorable trend that continues into early 2026. On a like-for-like basis, the group's EBITDAR rose by 19.2%, and EBITDA increased by 58% compared to 2024. This momentum did not weaken in the second half, with EBITDAR notably up 18.7% compared to the first half.

Engie
Engie is accelerating the development of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in two key European countries. The French energy group announced the acquisition of two BESS projects in Spain, totaling 278 MW / 1.1 GWh of capacity. These projects, which will allow for up to 4 hours of energy storage, are the largest currently under development in the country, where Engie already operates 1.8 GW of renewable capacity. Furthermore, Engie has begun construction of its first battery energy storage project in France. Located in the commune of Castelnau-d'Aude, in the Aude department, the site covers 2 hectares near a grid connection point.

Eramet
According to the Financial Times, the anchor shareholder of the French mining group has mandated Lazard to explore its options as Eramet undergoes a period of severe financial and governance tension. The Duval family, which holds 37% of Eramet's capital, has reportedly tasked Lazard with examining a possible sale of its stake, the Financial Times reports, citing several sources close to the matter. This deliberation comes as the group prepares a 500 million euro capital increase to shore up its financial position. Since the beginning of the year, the share price has plummeted by nearly 20%, bringing the market capitalization to approximately 1.4 billion euros.

LDC
During its fourth fiscal quarter (from December 2025 to February 2026), LDC's revenue came in at 1,975.8 million euros, up 12.5% on a reported basis. On a like-for-like basis, growth reached 9.2%, with sales of 1,916.2 million euros compared to 1,755.6 million euros a year earlier. On the back of this dynamic end to the fiscal year, LDC confirmed an upward revision of its annual targets, with revenue exceeding the 7 billion euro threshold and profitability expected to surpass initial expectations; the annual EBITDA target is now expected to "significantly exceed" the 560 million euros initially targeted.

Paref
Virgin Active has chosen "The Medelan" to launch its new "Social Wellness Club" concept over nearly 2,800 sqm in the heart of Milan. The PAREF group, which managed the redevelopment of the asset, is now responsible for its management. This new club will offer a unique and immersive experience, combining high-end fitness, wellness, coworking spaces, and areas dedicated to recovery and nutrition.

Rémy Cointreau
In a complex economic and geopolitical environment, Rémy Cointreau has presented an ambitious transformation plan: RC Forward. "This plan marks a decisive evolution, focused on reclaiming its markets and maximizing the potential of its brands," stated the family-owned liqueurs and spirits group. Additionally, the company unveiled the composition of a new organization.

The Group is establishing a steering committee within the executive committee, composed of five departments reporting to the CEO:

- Luca Marotta is appointed Deputy CEO of the group while retaining the Finance, IT, and Legal departments.

- Ian McLernon is appointed Group Markets Director and will oversee all regions, including a new Emerging Markets zone created to strengthen development in these high-potential markets.

- Mélanie Bulourde and Clarisse Petit will serve as Group Operations & CSR Director and Group Human Resources Director, respectively.

- A Brands Director will be appointed at a later date. In the interim, Franck Marilly will be responsible for this department.
The executive committee is also being expanded with the creation of two new departments: a Prestige department overseeing the Louis XIII, Telmont, and Maison Psyché brands, and a Chief Transformation Officer (CTO) for the duration of the transformation plan.

Riber
The specialist in molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) equipment for the semiconductor industry will deliver its annual results (to follow).

Sidetrade
Sidetrade unveils "O2C Intelligence 2030," a 4-year strategic plan that repositions it as an AI-native company dedicated to Order-to-Cash (O2C), with 3 new AI-native solutions to be launched by the end of the third quarter of 2026. The group will offer Aimie Agents, digital twins for every credit management role; the Agent Builder Studio, allowing clients to create their own agents; and Aimie IQ, a natural language interface for users, agents, and O2C applications.


SpineGuard
The company, which deploys its real-time surgical guidance technology, will publish its first-quarter revenue (to follow).

Technip Energies
Technip Energies has secured a substantial order from Commonwealth LNG, a Caturus group company, to continue the development of its 9.5 Mtpa liquefied natural gas (LNG) export project located in Cameron Parish, Louisiana (USA), marking a significant step in the project's progress toward a Final Investment Decision (FID). This new order is issued under the engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) contract previously signed with Commonwealth LNG.

data.title: Handelsbanken raises Ericsson price target, sees extended 5G cycle
data.description:
Handelsbanken has reiterated its buy rating and raised its price target for telecom equipment provider Ericsson to SEK 129 from SEK 106, according to a research note.

The bank anticipates a seasonally weak first quarter weighed down by currency headwinds, but forecasts currency-adjusted organic growth of approximately 3.7 percent and an EBITDA margin of around 11 percent. Analysts believe Ericsson maintains a technological lead in RAN and stands to benefit from a broader 5G rollout.

"Despite a share price increase of approximately 40 percent over the past 12 months, Ericsson stock is trading at an attractive valuation with an EV/EBIT of 9.5x (2026e) and a free cash flow yield of 6 percent," the analysts wrote.
